metadata.title:
GRAND JURY AGAIN


UNDER GORDON FIRE,

Attorneys Now Turned Inquisitors Of In
quisitors in Effort to Confiscate


Evidence.

pages.raw_text:
Northern in its combat against an
investigation of it's relations with
Judge M. J. Gordon, its former
counsel, now the subject of grand
jury inquiry. Under this ruling 1t
will not longer be necessary for
Louis Hill, president of the Great
Northern, to dodge the state of
Washington, and travel hundreds
of miles out Of the beaten path, for
fear of beinb compelled to produce
damaging documents in court.


A protecting mantale has been
thrown around tie operations Of
the Great Northern railway in
dealing with judges and legisla-
lures in the state Of Washington,
and the privilege right of the rail-
road to maintain under cover all
papers and correspondence relat-
ing to such activities remains un-
disturbed.


Judge Turner's plea in behalf of
the Great Northern was able and
ingenious and illustrated what fine
points of law the skilled advocate
can raise in defending the rights Of
a rich client under attack. Judge
Turner's plea went as far back as
the constitution of the United
States. which he showed was
framed to protect corporations in
distress.


. The court has ruled that the
prosecuting attorney is not permit
ted to take shorthand notes in the
grand jury room; that witnesses
before the grand jury can repeat
their testimony on the outside
when the same does not defeat the
ends of justice, and now that the
subpoena duces tecum can not be
invoked by the grand jury without
the consent of the attorneys of a
person under investigation and the
court.
